mortgage_to_gdp.description: Outstanding residential mortgage loans as % of national GDP

npl_ratio.description: Non-performing loan ratio (loans >90 days in arrears)

hh_debt_to_income.description: Household total debt as % of disposable income

hpi_volatility.description: Standard deviation of annual housing price index growth

singapore.summary: Multi-layered macroprudential approach with inter-agency coordination

uae.summary: 2013 regulations introduced post-Dubai crisis; improving effectiveness

india.summary: Indirect controls via risk-weights; no formal borrower-based limit

key_findings.title: Market Depth Disparities
key_findings.description: Singapore's mortgage market is 4-8x larger relative to GDP. Market depth driven by institutional maturity, not LTV strictness alone.

key_findings.title: Regulatory Effectiveness
key_findings.description: Singapore's multi-layered approach (TDSR + LTV + ABSD) most effective. UAE regulations working (NPL: 6.1% → 3.0%). India needs formal DSTI cap.

key_findings.title: Household Leverage Trajectories
key_findings.description: Singapore 111% (stable, well-buffered). UAE 25% (rising 1%/year). India 36% (moderate but invisible cumulative risk).

key_findings.title: Housing Price Volatility
key_findings.description: UAE 10.8% std dev (volatile, oil-sensitive). Singapore 4.2% (policy-managed). India 1.1% (stable, demand-driven).

key_findings.title: Institutional Maturity > Regulation Strictness
key_findings.description: Singapore's deep market works despite tight LTV due to public housing + savings culture + infrastructure. India's shallow market reflects weak institutions, not loose LTV.

key_findings.title: Timing of Regulation Matters
key_findings.description: UAE's 2013 regulations (pre-boom) prevented 2008 crash repeat. Lesson: India needs DSTI cap NOW, before next credit boom.

singapore: Incorporate climate risk into mortgage stress tests
uae: Proactively recalibrate DBR ahead of rate cuts (US peg effect)
Strengthen investment property LTV if mortgage growth >1% p.a.
Expand credit bureau coverage for fintech + all borrower types
Enforce off-plan LTV limits (50% cap) in high-growth segments
india: Implement 50% DSTI cap across all housing lenders (CRITICAL)
Fixed-rate mortgage incentives for affordable housing

insight_1.title: Institutional Maturity > Regulation Strictness
insight_1.key_point: Singapore's 46.9% M/GDP + 75% LTV works because of public housing, CPF savings, mature infrastructure. India's 11.1% + informal 80% LTV fails because institutions missing.
insight_1.lesson: Can't copy regulations without building foundational infrastructure first.

insight_2.title: Timing of Regulation Matters as Much as Content
insight_2.key_point: UAE introduced LTV/DBR in 2013 BEFORE boom accelerated. This allowed them to manage growth while preventing 2008 crisis repeat.
insight_2.lesson: India should implement DSTI cap NOW, not after next stress emerges.

insight_3.title: HPI Volatility = Structural Fragility Indicator
insight_3.key_point: UAE's 10.8% volatility reveals oil/expat sensitivity. Singapore's 4.2% shows policy cushioning. India's 1.1% shows stable fundamentals.
insight_3.lesson: Volatility tells you what risks to monitor; what external shocks matter.

insight_4.title: Institutional Infrastructure Must Precede Credit Expansion
insight_4.key_point: Correct sequence: credit bureaus → foreclosure laws → DSTI limits → deep markets. Wrong sequence (Dubai 2007): rapid credit → no safeguards → collapse.
insight_4.lesson: India at critical juncture. Build institutions now, deepen credit later.

main_risks: HDB resale price spillover to private market
singapore.mitigation: Maintain TDSR, monitor spillover, refine ABSD

main_risks: Potential demand spike from rate easing
uae.mitigation: Proactive DBR recalibration, expand credit coverage, enforce LTV limits

india.mitigation: Urgent: implement 50% DSTI cap, foreclosure reform, build MBS market

quantitative.source: IMF Financial Access Survey & Working Papers

limitations: Data comparability: Definitions vary by jurisdiction (all cross-validated)
Lack of microdata: Country-aggregate level, not borrower-level analysis
Limited causal analysis: Correlations and patterns, not econometric causality
3-country scope: Findings not tested on other ASEAN/GCC markets
future_research: Panel regression analysis (fixed-effects model) on full dataset
Stress test simulations (rate shock, HPI decline, income drop)
Expand to Malaysia, Saudi Arabia for regional comparison
Qualitative interviews with regulators and bankers
